speaker: interviewer
text: --June 29, 1995. Henry, did you ever see Goth commit a cruel act, while you were in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Personally?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I've seen him, once, putting his two dogs on-- on somebody that jumped him, and he probably was killed. Otherwise, personally I do not remember. I know there was a lot of shooting that he did. And then he has underlings who did his shootings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were executions a common occurrence in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a daily occurrence, almost. We did not have crematoria. We did not have gas chambers. Pits were dug-- people to be executed. And I must say here that he brought a lot of outside people in-- a lot of Polish people in-- political prisoners and so forth [INAUDIBLE]. They had to-- they had to strip. They had to jump into the pits. They were shot. And their gold teeth were knocked out. And then the next 10 or 15-- whatever it was-- had to do the same process, until they were all done. Then they poured gasoline over it, and they were ignited, and they were cremated. And the stench of-- of human bodies burning was over the camp for weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who knocked out and collected the gold teeth of these bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess prisoners. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually see these executions, or this was told to you by other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could see it from the-- no. I could see it. Not close by, but it was on a hill. I was in the back on a hill, where I could see when the trucks pulled up and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: During this period of time in Plaszow, who were some of the people-- of the prisoners-- that you had close contact with?

speaker: interviewee
text: During that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Plaszow, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was friendly with [NON-ENGLISH] who is mentioned in the-- in the book and who was Amon Goth's secretary, so to speak. We knew a lot of people. Working in the office, I knew a great number of people. And my parents, and my sister [INAUDIBLE] knew a lot of people. Mentioning names, I would have to refer to my correspondence, as there must be 100 names in there, when I inquired later what happened to him or what happened to her. Many of them were killed and were shot and deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: While [? you were in ?] [INAUDIBLE] did you know a Stern well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew Isac Stern and his brother Natan. Natan was a lawyer. Isac was an accountant. He was a very quiet man. He was always like a Talmudic scholar. He walked around with his-- with his hands in front of him, like this. I personally-- I knew him. We worked in the same office. I did not connect too well with him-- for no particular reason. It's just a question of [INAUDIBLE]. But I know that he was one of the-- one of the men around Schindler-- one of his inner sanctum. And he probably had an influence on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the Rosners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were two brothers-- Leopold and-- and Henry. One was a violin player. One was an accordion player. I knew them. I have Henry Rosner's signature on one of the-- the things I have, here-- a letter that my father had signed. They were good musicians. They had to play for the Germans all the time. There was one incident where they caught him in Plaszow. There was a lot of drinking. They did a lot of drinking. Goth caught them. That man for whom I worked-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- the one from our-- the one who took me out into town-- he called the musicians over. Goth called the musicians over, and Johann had to come to a party in his house. And the two musicians had played for hours and hours that night.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, Goth ordered that some-- some masons-- they had to-- to close up his door to Johann's villa-- a little house that he had. Put more bricks in and close it up so that he couldn't get out. [INAUDIBLE] Crazy things and funny things happened, too, once in a while. You know? During this period of time, were you aware of any observances of religious life in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of any-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Observances of religious life in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pla-- Well, in Krakow, where we were in-- in Krakow, or in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow-- I was not aware, but there was no official, organized Jewish life. But Orthodox Jews, I'm sure, kept their morning prayer and their evening prayer, and they kept on the holidays they try to keep some sort of-- some sort of--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in Krakow-- in the Krakow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were still some synagogues-- some temples in operation where we were-- where we were [INAUDIBLE] I think in the ghetto, I think there was one or two. They may have met informally to-- to pray. The High Holidays, I think, were in an informal way observed as well-- as well as could be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to Plaszow now. While you were in Plaszow, were there any particular acts of kindness that you feel helped you to survive in this difficult situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you that incident with my mother, with her teeth. That was definitely an act of kindness. [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Offhand, I could not think of any, really. There-- there was food smuggled into the camp. I know that. And I think I remember reading something that some money was smuggled in. And we knew the Wasserbergers-- that family had a very responsible men in their mill who sometimes bought us food into the ghetto. And I think  he brought some money in to be distributed among others. And I think that was done. The details, I do not remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When you were in Plaszow, were there any people who committed suicide, in these conditions, in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not aware of any. I'm sure there were some. I always read about so many suicides. I do not think it-- it was a suicidal atmosphere in Plaszow. I think the-- the will was to survive. Maybe because there were so many families together, that gave them the strength to-- to hold out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard or easy to keep in contact with your mother and sister and father at this time, in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well not with my father. With my mother and sister, they were in separate barracks. During the day, we did not see them. But the evening, curfew was at 9 o'clock. Sometimes we were able to go up to their barracks and see them, or they came down. And sometimes it was not allowed, but you did it anyway. And we-- we were in touch with them, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time that you were in Plaszow, were there additional groups of Jews that were coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Constantly. The small towns outside of Krakow were liquidated. And all these groups came in, and groups were sent out. And there-- there was a group of 1,000 or 1,200 Hungarian women that came in, in terrible shape. They had, I think, just a dress. Their hair was completely shorn. I don't remember whether they had shoes or clogs. But-- but just pathetic to see. [INAUDIBLE] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they became part of the camp. And there was a transport-- I'll just see, here-- 'In August 6, 1944, 5,000 women went to Auschwitz.' It was one transport where my mother-- my sister managed to pull my mother out. And 2,000 went to Stutthof, which was a concentration camp near-- near Danzig-- near Gdansk. 'These women were pushed into the ocean.' And, as far as we knew, there were no-- no survivors. Of course, I haven't seen it. It's what I-- common knowledge. 'The following day, 4,500 men went to Mauthausen.' That's where I had to pull my father out. And out of these, I understand 80 were alive as of December '45, when [INAUDIBLE] So these are some of the things that went on. The barracks had about 600 to 800 people in it. And after 9:00 you were not allowed to leave the barrack. Of course, there were no facilities in there. They had a big bucket in the-- in the hall of the barrack, and everybody went there-- did whatever they had to do. And, you know, the day it was taken out. It was a messy, stinking situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these deporta-- what did these deportations, these constant deportations, indicate to you about the future of this labor camp-- of Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not-- we were concerned, obviously. You know, I-- I guess, in retrospective, after a while you become numb. You-- you try to survive that one day, you've got it made, and then you keep on for the next day. You see what happens then.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first become aware of the existence of Schindler's list?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was in August '44, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you became aware of this existence of this list?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we know-- it was a well-known thing, because the-- the people from Schindler's list-- I think Schindler-- now that you'd mention-- Schindler's Jews from his camp came back into Plaszow. So they were there again. And then it was known. You know, in a ghetto, and in a prison, you know what's going on, no matter what.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you and your family get on this list?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did we--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you and your family get added to Schindler's list?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have a positive answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was a combination of luck, and luck, and luck, and maybe the fact that I worked in the office there and spoke German and could-- could do a lot of the things that Polish people just could not do. I think that had lot to do with it. And Schindler tried not to pull families apart. And since my mother and my sister were with us. So somehow we got on Schindler's list. Definitely, I cannot tell you. I cannot tell you I went to somebody and said, hey, put me on Schindler's list, and they said, well, give me $10,000, I'll put you on Schindler's list. That didn't exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that you and your family were on this list?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, through the office, we-- we heard. And I want to point out that that was not necessarily the salvation-- Schindler's list. We did not really know whether this would succeed or not. Of course, it did not exist in the annals of-- of the concentration camp that a factory could request 1,000 people by name. They could say, I want 50 electricians and 100 plumbers. But they said I want Jack Miller and Max Smith-- this did not exist. And-- and that was Schindler's greatest accomplishment, that he was able to do this. He said, these are-- he had to make ammunition. He had to build up an ammunition factory. These are his people. These are the people who are qualified to do this job. And if the German government wants Schindler to do-- to do his job for the German army, he needs these people. And I guess, with the support of diamonds and liquor and so on, this went over. He had great trouble with the women, to getting them out, because the-- the commandant in Auschwitz didn't know of-- didn't want to know of it. And I heard-- whether it's true are not, I don't know-- that he had to go to Berlin and pull strings there, and to finally get them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go back a step to your-- your family actually leaving Plaszow to be transported to Schindler's new factory in Czechoslovakia. When did you and your father leave [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left October 15th, my father and I. We went to Gross-Rosen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an extermination camp in eastern Germany, in which during the war I understand one and a half million people were killed. We spent three days there. We got there. We were received with beating. The Germans didn't do the beating. The prisoners had to do the beating. There were prisoners of all countries there. We were stripped right away. We had to strip. We had to stand on a large assembly place. It was October-- the end of October. And Europe can be very cold. It was a rainy night. We stood there all night, until 3 o'clock in the morning, in the rain. And normally we would catch pneumonia. None of us did catch pneumonia. Then they took us to a hot shower. They shaved us from head to toe. And, wet and barefoot as we were, we went to some magazine where they threw some shoes, pants, shirt, and a cap at us. And then, after three days, we were finally sent out there. And there was a quarries where the prisoners worked, in-- in Gross-Rosen. We didn't work during these three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now these clothes that they gave you at this time, after being shaved and et cetera-- was there-- how would you describe those clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here's something I do not quite-- quite remember. The uniform I have-- it is my understanding we got it in Gross-Rosen. The-- the notes that I have is that we got some recycled civilian clothes. But I remember we got wooden clogs in Gross-Rosen. And we got-- so I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. At this point, in Gross-Rosen, you were assigned a number.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, do you recall that number?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was assigned there in Gross-Rosen-- I'm quite sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What do you remember most about those three days in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was just one big-- big impression-- is the way we slept. They put us in a big room. The first one had to sit against the wall, like [INAUDIBLE] sitting here now. The next one had to sit back, push up, like this, back to him, and the next one. So, a long row, all sitting, spread out like this. And you want to go to the bathroom, if you wanted to climb over-- over 20 other people in front of you-- forget it. It was a very terrible camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you given to eat, during those three days?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Probably some-- some soup, maybe a little bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the trip from Gross-Rosen, when you left, to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll start from Plaszow to Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put in cattle cars-- probably around 100. The window was covered with barbed wire. The doors were sealed. And we went into-- And on we went. And basically the same way, though. There was bucket or a pail in the corner of-- of the car. And which overflowed, of course, very soon. And that was it. That's how we went to Brunnlitz, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days did it take to go from Gross-Rosen to Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it wasn't that much. I think it was a one-day trip or so. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day trip. Now what did you-- what were your first impressions of Brunnlitz, when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a march from the railroad station in. I don't really know. I don't really know. We were glad to be-- of course, you know, the-- the-- Schindler's reputation was known to us. And so we were confident. And nobody was beating us when we got there. Nobody was screaming at us when we got there. And so I don't really-- I can't answer that question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Schindler greet you, when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I know he greeted the women. But I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Once you got into Brunnlitz, where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brunnlitz-- the factory was a big, big factory hall-- like a big factory hall. On top of the factory hall, upstairs, there was, like, two or three big rooms. One was for the men. And the other one, later, for the women. Where they had-- First, nothing was there. It was very primitive when we got there. After a week or so, we got bunks there-- two high, but individual bunks, at least, or bunks with two. And upstairs-- we slept upstairs. And the kitchen was upstairs. The latrine was outside. You had to go out of the building. And, you know, there was another building where the office was and where the German soldiers had their quarters. And Schindler himself had a-- an apartment, right out from the factory hall to the apartment. He had to go through the factor. Hall, so we saw him and his wife all the time and any visitors he would get. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a-- it was a textile plant when we moved in. And we had to dismantle the machines, and we had to install ammunition plants, and ammunition factory. We made a certain-- a certain ammunition part. And we had that, and a few German engineers. And then the Jews had to do the labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother and sister tell you about their trip from Plaszow to Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they left-- they left Plaszow on August 21,. And they-- they arrived in Brunnlitz on November 14the. That means they were three weeks on the-- on the road. They arrived in terrible shape. You could hardly recognize them. And they told terrible things about-- about Auschwitz. My mother was sent-- as a-- as an older woman-- was sent to what they call a Greisenheim-- an old-age home-- which every few days was emptied out to the gas chambers or the crematorium. A few hours before-- before Schindler's people finally went, my sister was able to pull her out of there. And I can-- I can imagine how she worked on it to get her out. They were in bad shape. But they-- they were together. And eventually they came back to their-- came back to their strength.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think Schindler was able to get the women out of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, 'how,' I should say.

speaker: interviewee
text: How.

speaker: interviewer
text: How.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to rely on what I read in the book and what I heard. Personally, I don't know. I think he tried to bribe-- again, this is-- I don't know [INAUDIBLE]-- bribe the commander, and he  didn't go for it. I think he sent his secretary over to work with the-- with the commander there, and that didn't work. And I heard he had to go to Berlin. Somehow, he still had-- among the Nazis, he had-- he had influence. And that's how he finally got them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel you were in any danger, while you were in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Were we in any danger while we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel you were in danger, while you were in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only from Allied bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you so confident, at that point, that you were out of danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do to make you feel confident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, the Germans-- the German camp people-- he would not let the German camp people into the factory. They could guard the-- the outside, the yard and everything. But not inside. So we were his-- his Jews-- his children, and his coworkers. And he always promised, with him you-- you will survive the war. And he built up his magazines with-- warehouses-- with-- with material and with fabric. I think I mentioned this before. And he tried to get as much food as he could. And-- and Mrs. Schindler tried to help with the sick and food-- get food in the-- you know-- through-- through the connections she had. And we were confident.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe a typical day in Brunnlitz for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. We got up at 7 o'clock or 6 o'clock. We went down to the latrine. And we went up, and there was roll call. It was, I think, in our room. You stood-- stood there. And we had some breakfast. And everybody went down into the hall and the shop. My mother and my sister worked as [INAUDIBLE] jobs-- quality control. In other words, we didn't manufacture anything yet, but there was a quality control. And my father did something similar to it, too. And I think my father worked in the kitchen a little while. And I worked up in Schindler's office-- not in-- not in the German office, but in Schindler's factory office civilian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing in his office?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I expected to do some paperwork and typing and so on, but it's not what I did. They made me a cost accountant. I had to do cost accountancy [CHUCKLES] for a product that we never really manufactured. [LAUGH] So it was sort of a-- sort of a job-- an easy job I suppose. But, again, you were always the-- the German guards-- they had quarters right in back of our office. And we always had to-- we heard when they had to march and sing-- march around in there and sing these Nazi songs. And-- and there was a German commander, a Sturmfuhrer Leopold, like a Captain Leopold, who was in charge there. But I think Schindler had him-- Schindler arranged, just when it came to the end of the war, that he was transferred out of this-- out of Brunnlitz. And that got him out of the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the status of the war, while you were at Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, prisons have no walls. There was a radio mechanic, a Jewish radio mechanic, who repaired the radios for the Jews. Of course, we had no radios. But when he had to repair radios-- the German army, three times a day, gave out an army report status-- where they were. Since I knew good German, and knew a lot about Germany, and I knew German shorthand, I took these reports down as well as I can. And then I showed it to the other here, there, here. And then the word spread around. We knew what went on. For instance, I came from Dresden. Dresden was destroyed in February '45 by bombing in two nights. You may have heard about it. We knew about it because a newspaper was smuggled in from Frankfurt that-- that wrote an article about this. So we knew what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the Russian front, at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew the Russian front was advancing. There were air raids-- frequent air raids, and the alarms-- the sirens went off. And that was one of our concerns-- we shouldn't be bombed, either by the Russians or by the Americans. And but it so happened where we were liberated, Brunnlitz was in that part of Germany, of Europe-- to the right, to the east, were the Russians. To the west were the Allies, already. They really had nowhere to send us anymore. And it came to the last few days. The  Germans packed themselves up and-- and left. But we were just lucky that we were not killed just before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your opinion of Schindler, during-- during these months you spent in Brunnlitz? Well, to us Schindler-- Schindler was a hero. Now, his reputation was terrible. You know-- you know all about being a womanizer, and a scandal, and the black-market deal, and everything. But he protected us. So he never yelled at us. He never hit anybody. He never screamed at anyone. He never shot anyone. He was our protector. During this period of time in Brunnlitz, besides your family was there anyone else that you would say you were very close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time you were at Brunnlitz, was there anyone else outside of your family who you felt very close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew them all. It was a small camp. I-- I can't answer that question. Not any one. There was a Dr. Schlang, from Vienna, who was a lawyer from Vienna, who was close to my father, I remember. And he went back to-- to Krakow later. And if I go through the list, there are 30 or 40 names that I-- that I recognize, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the activities that Schindler's wife carried out in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she helped in the sick bay. She would walk in there. She would try to get food and-- and medicines for the sick. And she worked in this field. I don't know whether I mentioned it, that-- I think it was in February-- that a transport of 200 people or so came in these frozen-- frozen cattle cars when the east camps were liquidated-- about two cars, I think-- that were going all over the country. No concentration camp wanted to let them in. And it was-- finally came to Brunnlitz I think they were for two weeks-- a week or two weeks. These cattle cars, in bitter cold. And-- and Schindler ordered to let them in. And I understand, Mrs. Schindler wasn't there. Mrs. Schindler called her husband, wherever he was, and he gave an order to let them in. We could not even open the cattle cars. They had to be-- we had to have torches to open them up. And many of them were-- were just dead already. They were-- they were frozen to death. And whoever could get them-- [INAUDIBLE] out, there were maybe 100 or 120 that we took in. And they had to nourish them, and to bring them back to health.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
